title: The History of the Internet
subtitle: From packet switching to the platform era — told as a story
description: A narrative full-length course tracing the people and decisions behind the internet, from 1960s Cold War research labs to the centralised platforms of today.
targetAudience: Anyone who has ever wondered how we got here
objectives: Trace the lineage from ARPANET to the modern web
Understand why the internet is decentralised — and why the platforms aren't
Recognise the key design choices that still shape how we use it
See how policy decisions, not just technology, drove outcomes

modules.title: Before the Net
modules.description: The Cold War labs where the idea was born.
objectives: Understand packet switching as a response to a specific problem
Meet the people whose ideas made it possible

lessons.title: The Nuclear Question
lessons.description: Paul Baran's 1964 memo and the problem it was solving.
lessons.content: In 1964, Paul Baran at RAND was asked a chilling question: how could the United States maintain communications after a nuclear first strike? His answer — breaking messages into small pieces that could find their own way across a damaged network — became the packet-switching idea.
keyPoints: The problem was survivability, not speed
Baran's memo sat unused for years before anyone implemented it
Centralised networks had a single point of failure the Pentagon couldn't accept

lessons.title: The Licklider Vision
lessons.description: The man who pitched a network of computers as a network of minds.
lessons.content: J.C.R. Licklider ran the computing division at ARPA in the early 1960s and wrote a series of memos imagining what he called an 'intergalactic computer network.' His titles were grandiose; his ideas — resource sharing, remote work, conversational computing — turned out mostly correct.
keyPoints: Licklider funded the researchers who would build ARPANET
His memos read as startlingly accurate predictions of the 2020s
Government research funding was essential — no company would have taken the risk

lessons.title: Building ARPANET
lessons.description: The first four nodes, and the first crash.
lessons.content: On October 29, 1969, a graduate student in Los Angeles tried to log into a computer in Stanford by typing 'LOGIN'. The system crashed after 'LO'. That was the first internet message — and the first internet bug.
keyPoints: Interface Message Processors were the first routers
The original four nodes were UCLA, SRI, UCSB, and Utah
Early ARPANET was built by grad students under remarkable autonomy

modules.title: Finding a Language
modules.description: How TCP/IP became the lingua franca of every network.
objectives: See why a common protocol mattered

lessons.title: Cerf and Kahn
lessons.description: A design on a napkin in a San Francisco hotel.
lessons.content: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn sketched TCP/IP in 1974, partly on the back of an envelope. Their insight was that the network should be stupid — it just moved packets — while the computers at the edges did the clever work. That inversion is still the foundation today.
keyPoints: The end-to-end principle shaped every layer above
TCP guarantees delivery; IP just routes
Separating concerns made independent evolution possible

lessons.title: The 1983 Switch
lessons.description: Flag Day, and why it almost broke everything.
lessons.content: On January 1, 1983, ARPANET switched from its old NCP protocol to TCP/IP overnight. It was called Flag Day and it worked — mostly. It remains one of the few times a major network transitioned all at once, rather than gradually.
keyPoints: Migrations this abrupt are now considered bad practice
The switch enabled the network to scale beyond ARPANET
TCP/IP's openness let other networks adopt it and connect

lessons.title: DNS
lessons.content: Early networks kept a single text file of every computer's name and address. When that got too big, Paul Mockapetris designed the Domain Name System in 1983 — a distributed, hierarchical directory service. It's still what turns 'example.com' into an IP address.
keyPoints: The hosts.txt file couldn't scale past a few hundred machines
DNS is hierarchical, distributed, and cached — all by design
The root servers are a geopolitically sensitive set of thirteen clusters

modules.title: The Web Arrives
modules.description: Tim Berners-Lee, CERN, and the idea that changed everything.
objectives: Understand hypertext as an idea older than the web
See why the web won over competitors

lessons.title: Hypertext
lessons.description: Bush, Nelson, and the idea you could link anything to anything.
lessons.content: Vannevar Bush imagined a mechanical linking device in 1945. Ted Nelson coined 'hypertext' in 1965 and spent decades on Xanadu, a system he never shipped. Tim Berners-Lee's genius was knowing what to leave out.
keyPoints: Hypertext was a 40-year idea before the web
Earlier systems tried for perfection and shipped nothing
Berners-Lee's web was intentionally simpler — and worked

lessons.title: CERN, 1989
lessons.description: The memo that changed the world.
lessons.content: Tim Berners-Lee's 1989 proposal, titled 'Information Management: A Proposal,' got a handwritten 'vague but exciting' from his manager. He took that as a yes and built HTML, HTTP, and the first browser in his spare time over the next year.
keyPoints: The web was built to solve CERN's internal documentation problem
Berners-Lee could have patented the web — he chose not to
The original browser was also an editor; the read/write split came later

lessons.title: Mosaic and the Explosion
lessons.description: The browser that put the web on every desktop.
lessons.content: By 1993, NCSA Mosaic gave the web inline images and point-and-click navigation. The web user base grew 10x that year. Mosaic's team then commercialised as Netscape — and the browser wars began.
keyPoints: Mosaic didn't invent the web; it made it ordinary people could use it
Inline images made the web visual, not just textual
The Netscape IPO in 1995 kicked off the dot-com era

modules.title: Boom, Bust, and Platform
modules.description: The bubble, the crash, and what came after.
objectives: Understand the dot-com bubble in its context

lessons.title: The Bubble
lessons.content: Between 1995 and early 2000, internet stocks rose to levels no earnings could justify. Pets.com spent more on Super Bowl advertising than it earned in revenue. The NASDAQ peaked in March 2000 and then fell 78% over the next 30 months.
keyPoints: Most of the bubble-era predictions were directionally correct — just 10 years too early
The crash destroyed fortunes but left the infrastructure built
Amazon, eBay, and Google survived; nearly everyone else didn't

lessons.title: Web 2.0
lessons.content: After the crash, a new pattern emerged: sites where users created the content. YouTube, Facebook, Wikipedia, Flickr — these were the products. The network effect was the moat.
keyPoints: User-generated content scaled in ways paid content never could
Network effects became the central economic logic
Open APIs accelerated the ecosystem — briefly

lessons.title: The Mobile Turn
lessons.description: How the iPhone reshaped the internet.
lessons.content: The 2007 iPhone and the 2008 App Store quietly rewrote the rules. Mobile traffic overtook desktop by 2016. Apps replaced browsers for most activities, handing two companies veto power over half the internet.
keyPoints: The mobile internet is not just a smaller version of desktop
The app-store model re-introduced centralised gatekeeping
The open web now competes with closed platforms it partly created

modules.title: Today's Questions
modules.description: The architecture of the next decade.
objectives: Form a view on where the trajectory is heading

lessons.title: The Platform Problem
lessons.description: When a handful of companies mediate the rest.
lessons.content: A few companies now sit between billions of users and the rest of the internet. That position produces great products, great profits, and great concentrations of power — and no one has a clean answer for what to do about it.
keyPoints: Network effects reward consolidation
Antitrust frameworks from the 1890s don't fit digital markets neatly
The EU and US have drifted apart on regulatory approach

lessons.title: Decentralisation, Again
lessons.description: Why every few years someone tries to rebuild the open web.
lessons.content: From Mastodon to blockchains to personal data pods, every few years a new wave promises to restore the decentralised internet. The track record is mixed — the hard part has never been the technology.
keyPoints: Decentralisation is hard because centralised services are often more convenient
Protocols beat platforms when the protocol is genuinely open
The cycle repeats — because the underlying pull of centralisation is real

lessons.title: What the Story Leaves Us
lessons.description: Reading the past to read the present.
lessons.content: The internet wasn't inevitable. Every layer was a choice someone made, often for reasons that looked narrow at the time. The current internet is also a choice — and the next one will be too.
keyPoints: Design decisions made decades ago still constrain us today
Policy choices shape technology at least as much as the reverse
Knowing the history is a prerequisite for thinking about what comes next

quiz.question: What problem was packet switching originally designed to solve?
options: Survivable communications after catastrophic damage to the network
quiz.explanation: Paul Baran's 1964 RAND memo proposed packet switching specifically as a way to keep communications functioning even after large parts of the network were destroyed.
